title: American coffee cake
subtitle: Cinnamon streusel-topped sponge for eating with coffee
excerpt: A simple vanilla sponge baked in a square tin with a thick cinnamon and brown sugar streusel crumble pressed into the top before baking. The streusel bakes into a crisp, buttery layer. No coffee in the batter — the name refers to the occasion, not the flavour.
sourceNotes: American coffee cake is documented in domestic American cookbooks from the mid-nineteenth century onward, many available through the Internet Archive. The term refers not to a coffee-flavoured cake but to a cake intended to be eaten with coffee, a tradition in American household entertaining. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the chapter on cakes documents a crumb-topped sponge baked in a square tin as a standard cake suitable for informal service with tea or coffee. The streusel topping, of German origin, was documented in American domestic manuals from the late nineteenth century as a standard topping for coffee cakes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into portions and freeze in a bag. The streusel stays reasonably crisp after defrosting at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps wrapped at room temperature for 3 days. The streusel softens slightly on day two but remains flavourful.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The streusel browns faster than the cake below it — check at 30 minutes and cover with foil if the top is darkening

glossaryTerms.definition: A crumbly topping made by rubbing cold butter into a mixture of flour, sugar, and spice until the mixture forms irregular clumps. The clumps bake into a crisp, buttery layer on top of the cake. The ratio of butter to dry ingredients determines the cluster size: more butter produces larger, more cohesive clumps; less butter produces fine, sandy crumbs.

content.text: An American coffee cake is a square-tin, one-layer affair — not a layer cake, not a celebration cake, just a simple sponge baked at home for a weekend morning. The streusel is the point: it bakes into a buttery, cinnamon-scented crust on top of the plain sponge below. The combination of soft cake and crisp, sugared topping is the whole appeal.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} for the streusel, {{light-brown-sugar}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}} in a bowl. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}} for the streusel and rub it in with your fingertips until the mixture forms irregular clumps. This is the
. Refrigerate while you make the cake batter.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Butter and line the square tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} for the cake with the {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, then the {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}} for the cake, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} alternately with the {{whole-milk}}, beginning and ending with the flour. Pour into the tin.

Scatter the streusel evenly over the top of the batter and press it in lightly. Bake for 35–38 minutes until a skewer inserted into the centre of the cake (not the streusel) comes out clean. The streusel should be deep brown and crisp. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes before cutting into squares.
